title: Integrated squeezed light sources for two-mode entanglement in thin-film lithium niobate
authors: Philipp Lohmann, Renato R. Domeneguetti, Daniel Wendland, Alessandro Perino, Tobias Egebjerg, Liam McRae, Jonas S. Neergaard-Nielsen, Wolfram H. P. Pernice, Ulrik L. Andersen
abstract: Scalable generation of nonclassical light sources on an integrated platform is a key requirement for photonic quantum information processing. In particular, realizing multiple indistinguishable squeezed light sources on a single chip is an essential step toward continuous-variable quantum computing. Here, we demonstrate the fabrication of two indistinguishable and independently controllable optical parametric oscillators on a thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform. The device design focuses on reproducibility, independent tunability, and compatibility with larger telecom-wavelength continuous-variable photonic circuits. We observe up to 0.5 dB of directly measured squeezing below the shot-noise level from each source. By interfering the two modes on a beam splitter, we generate an EPR-type two-mode squeezed state and verify continuous-variable entanglement through violation of the Duan-Simon inseparability criterion. This is the first demonstration of two independently tunable squeezed-light sources on a single TFLN chip and their use for generating continuous-variable entanglement.
summary: Researchers have successfully integrated two independently tunable squeezed-light sources on a single thin-film lithium niobate chip. By interfering these sources, they demonstrated the generation of continuous-variable entanglement. This provides a scalable pathway for building complex, integrated quantum photonic circuits.
main_problem: The need for scalable, integrated, and independently controllable nonclassical light sources on a single platform for continuous-variable quantum information processing.
main_result: Demonstration of two indistinguishable and independently tunable optical parametric oscillators (OPOs) on a single thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) chip, used to generate EPR-type two-mode entanglement.
method: Fabrication of TFLN waveguides with periodic poling, use of on-chip micro-heaters for resonance tuning, and interference of the two squeezed modes on an off-chip beam splitter followed by balanced homodyne detection.
model_or_system: An integrated photonic platform based on X-cut thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) on SiO2, featuring two single-resonant OPOs utilizing spontaneous parametric down-conversion at telecom wavelengths (1550 nm).
key_observables: Squeezing levels (dB), Duan-Simon inseparability criterion value, quadrature variances, and OPO pump power thresholds.
important_parameters: Signal wavelength ~1550 nm, pump wavelength ~775 nm, squeezing up to 0.5 dB, total efficiency ~0.53-0.54, and OPO thresholds between 15-23 W.
main_assumptions: The system operates in the low-pump regime well below the OPO threshold.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic and experimental setup; Figure 2 displays the fabricated chip layout and SEM images of waveguides; Figure 3 presents the squeezing spectrum, power dependence, and quadrature variance measurements.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scalability problem, describes the TFLN device architecture and fabrication, details the experimental tuning and entanglement generation setup, presents the measurement of squeezing and entanglement, and concludes with limitations and future directions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it demonstrates a scalable hardware building block for continuous-variable quantum computing and large-scale Gaussian photonic circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scheme of the integrated two-source squeezed-light device and off-chip two-mode entanglement generation. Two single-resonant OPOs are fabricated on a TFLN chip. Each OPO contains a periodically poled section, an on-chip heater for resonance tuning, and a dichroic directional coupler for separating pump and signal light. The squeezed outputs are collected from the chip and interfered on an off-chip beam splitter to generate a two-mode squeezed state. Seed inputs are used to lock the squeezing quadratures. The dashed circle represents the shot noise variance reference level.

FIG. 2. a) Optical microscope image during the fabrication taken before printing the 3D-couplers on the chip. Details of the integrated platform: i) SEM image of the fiber-to-chip couplers, ii) SEM image of the dichroic directional coupler used to separate pump and signal light, and iii) piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM) image of the periodically poled lithium niobate before photonic fabrication, confirming uniform domain inversion. The waveguide is subsequently etched in the region where the periodic poling has approximately 50 % duty cycle. b) Camera image of the fabricated chip showing the fiber array interface and electrical probe contacts. c) Measured transmission spectra of the two...

FIG. 3. a) Schematics for two-mode squeezing generation from the integrated circuit. Setup The fiber-based setup contains the laser source and the light distribution of both wavelength bands at 1550 (yellow) and 775 nm (blue) wavelengths. Light is manipulated using VOAs, phase shifters and polarization controllers. Chip Two seed and two pump sources are passed to the integrated circuit, using a fiber array for the optical and an electrical probe for the electrical signals. The global temperature is controlled with a peltier element.
